response: The global food system faces unprecedented pressure from population growth, climate change, and resource constraints. By 2050, the world’s population is projected to reach 9.7 billion, requiring about a 60% increase in food production, while climate change and extreme weather are already reducing yields. Agriculture contributes roughly 25% of greenhouse gas emissions, water scarcity is worsening in major farming areas (including faster depletion of the Ogallala Aquifer), and soil degradation has reduced productivity on 33% of the world’s arable land; additionally, about one-third of food is lost or wasted between farm and fork. Researchers and policymakers propose solutions such as precision agriculture with AI and sensors, vertical farming, alternative proteins, drought-resistant crops, and regenerative farming, alongside policy reforms—while the key challenge is scaling these approaches so food stays affordable for the world’s poorest.
